summary: Spolsky argues that human aptitude follows a bell curve, with each increase in technical complexity dramatically reducing the number of people who can participate. He illustrates this with a striking progression from TV usage (98%) down to ATL programming (0.01%). The core insight is that even small reductions in complexity yield disproportionately large gains in audience. He also briefly comments on shrinking cubicles as a recruiting disadvantage and links to his UI design series and an Excel scheduling article.
key_points: Human aptitude for technology follows a steep drop-off curve, not a gentle slope, with each layer of complexity eliminating most of the remaining population
Making software even 10% easier to use can increase the potential user base by 50% due to where people cluster on the aptitude curve
Companies willing to invest in humane office space gain a recruiting advantage over those following the trend of shrinking cubicles
money_quotes: Maybe 98% of your friends are smart enough to use a television set. About 70% of them can use Windows. 15% can use Linux. 1% can program.

The effect of this sharp drop-off is that whenever you "lower the bar" by even a small amount, making your program, say, 10% easier to use, you dramatically increase the number of people who can use it, say, by 50%.
If you're willing to spend more to provide humane office space, you should have no problem recruiting people.
key_insight: Small reductions in software complexity yield disproportionately large increases in the number of people who can use it, because human aptitude drops off sharply rather than gradually.
